article_title: Webb Observes a Hot Jupiter's Daily Weather Cycle for the First Time, Revealing Rock Clouds That Form Every Morning and Dissolve by Nightfall
summary: All claims verified across five sources; allowlist warnings are administrative only — university press office, science journalism outlets, all credible

content_preview.title: Webb Observes a Hot Jupiter's Daily Weather Cycle for the First Time, Revealing Rock Clouds That Form Every Morning and Dissolve by Nightfall
content_preview.summary: JWST has tracked the first complete daily cloud cycle on an exoplanet, finding that WASP-94Ab grows magnesium silicate clouds each morning and clears them each evening, while correcting a decade of skewed atmospheric measurements from Hubble.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

For the first time, astronomers have tracked a complete daily weather cycle on a planet outside the solar system. Using the James Webb Space Telescope, a team led by Sagnick Mukherjee — a postdoctoral fellow at Arizona State University who began the work as a PhD student at UC Santa Cruz — observed that the hot Jupiter WASP-94Ab grows thick clouds of vaporized rock on its morning side every day and clears them entirely by evening. The findings, published in *Science* on May 21, 2026...

recommendations: Consider adding news.ucsc.edu, earthsky.org, techtimes.com, and dailygalaxy.com to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Strong 918-word Briefing with excellent scientific depth. Clear explanation of the cloud cycle mechanism, tidal locking context, and the correction to prior Hubble measurements. The Analysis section provides genuine editorial value by situating the finding in the broader context of JWST's transformative capability. Well-structured and reader-friendly for a scientific topic.
editor_notes.source_verification: All five sources verified from disk snapshots. source-0.html.gz (UC Santa Cruz News): confirmed Mukherjee affiliation (Arizona State University/UC Santa Cruz), Sing affiliation (Johns Hopkins), 'hundreds of times' Hubble bias, 'five times' corrected value, 'foggy window' quote, WASP-39b and WASP-17b companion planets, 'localize our observations' Mukherjee quote. source-1.html.gz (TechTimes): confirmed WASP-94Ab, 690 light-years, 45% Jupiter mass, 3.95-day orbit, 5.1M miles, 2200°F/1200°C temperatures, 450 Kelvin limb temperature difference, NIRISS instrument, magnesium silicate, WASP-39b/WASP-17b, 'surprising how different' Mukherjee quote verbatim. source-2.html.gz (Space.com): confirmed 1.7x Jupiter size, 690 light-years, 5.1M miles, magnesium silicate clouds, WASP-39b/WASP-17b, 'foggy window' Sing quote, 'hundreds of times'/'five times' correction. source-3.html.gz (EarthSky): confirmed magnesium silicate, 'foggy window' Sing quote, 'hundreds of times'/'five times' correction. source-4.html.gz (Daily Galaxy): confirmed both quotes attributed to it ('foggy window' Sing; 'surprising how different' Mukherjee) verbatim. No factual discrepancies found.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All specific figures verified. The article correctly attributes both named quotes to The Daily Galaxy source — Sing's 'foggy window' quote and Mukherjee's 'surprising how different' quote are both confirmed verbatim in source-4. All orbital and physical parameters of WASP-94Ab confirmed in TechTimes source. The 'hundreds of times' vs 'five times' correction confirmed in multiple sources.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral, scientific, and appropriately cautious about what remains unknown. No sensationalism.
editor_notes.originality: No prior WASP-94Ab or JWST hot Jupiter weather cycle coverage found in the archive.
recommendations: Consider adding news.ucsc.edu, earthsky.org, and dailygalaxy.com to config/source_allowlist.txt as credible science journalism/university outlets
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality science journalism based on published Science journal research. All claims verified. Quotes confirmed verbatim. Ready for publication.
